title: Witch Week - Chapter 7 (Fragment 3)
text:
Charles sat and brooded, rather surprised at the things going on in his mind. He was not frightened at all now. He seemed to be accepting the fact he was a witch quite calmly after all. No one had noticed. They all thought the witch was Nan Pilgrim, because of her name, which suited Charles very well. But the really strange thing was the way he had stopped being worried by the witch he had seen being burned. He tried remembering him, cautiously at first, then boldly, when he found it did not bother him. Then he went on to the second witch, who came over the wall. Neither troubled him now. They were in the past: they were gone. It was like having a toothache that suddenly stops. In the peace that came with this, Charles saw that his mind must have been trying to tell him he was going to grow up a witch. And now that he knew, it stopped nagging him. Then, to see if this made him frightened, he thought of inquisitors. It hurts to be burned, he thought, and looked at his fat blister. It had taught him something after
all. And that was: Don’t get found out.

Good, thought Charles. And turned his mind to what he was going to do to Simon Silverson. Dan Smith next, but Simon definitely first. What could he do to Simon that would be worth nearly a whole term’s pocket money? It was difficult. It had to be something bad enough, and yet with no connection with Charles. Charles was quite stumped at first. He wanted it to be artistic. He wanted Simon to suffer. He wanted everyone else to know about it, but not to know it was Charles who did it. What could he do?

The last lesson before lunch was the daily PE. Today, it was the boys’ turn in the gym. They were to climb ropes too. Charles sat by the wallbars and pretended to tie his gym shoe. Unlike Nan, he could get up a rope if he wanted, but he did not want to. He wanted to sit and think what to do to Simon. Simon, of course, was one of the first to the ceiling. He saw Charles and shouted something down. The result was that someone from 6C came and dug Charles in the back.

“Simon says you’re to stop lazing about.”

“Simon says that, does he?” said Charles. He stood up. He was inspired. It was something Brian had said last night. That game, Simon Says. Suppose it was not just a game. Suppose everything Simon said really came true. At the very worst it ought to be pretty funny. At best, people might even think Simon was a witch.

Charles went up a rope. He dragged himself up it, nice and slow and gentle, so that he could go on thinking. He was obviously not going to be able to stand anywhere near Simon to put the spell on him. Someone would notice. But instinct told Charles that this was not the kind of magic you could work at a distance. It was too strong and personal. What he needed, in order to do it safely, was something which was not Simon himself, but something which belonged to Simon so personally that any witchcraft worked on it would work on Simon at the same time—a detachable piece of Simon, really. What removable parts had Simon? Teeth, toenails, fingernails, hair? He could hardly go up to Simon and pull any of those off him. Wait a minute! Hair. Simon combed his hair this morning. With any luck, there might be some hair stuck in Simon’s comb.

vocabulary.definition: consenting to receive or undertake something offered
vocabulary.example: He seemed to be accepting the fact he was a witch quite calmly after all.

vocabulary.definition: having or showing creative skill or taste
vocabulary.example: He wanted it to be artistic.

vocabulary.definition: a small bubble on the skin filled with serum
vocabulary.example: It hurts to be burned, he thought, and looked at his fat blister.

vocabulary.definition: in a courageous and confident manner
vocabulary.example: He tried remembering him, cautiously at first, then boldly, when he found it did not bother him.

vocabulary.definition: to think deeply about something that makes one unhappy
vocabulary.example: Charles sat and brooded, rather surprised at the things going on in his mind.

vocabulary.definition: in a quiet and peaceful manner
vocabulary.example: He seemed to be accepting the fact he was a witch quite calmly after all.

vocabulary.definition: with care to avoid danger or mistakes
vocabulary.example: He tried remembering him, cautiously at first, then boldly, when he found it did not bother him.

vocabulary.definition: the upper interior surfaces of a room
vocabulary.example: Simon, of course, was one of the first to the ceiling.

vocabulary.definition: to go up something
vocabulary.example: They were to climb ropes too.

vocabulary.definition: to untangle or arrange hair with a comb
vocabulary.example: Simon combed his hair this morning.

vocabulary.definition: to separate or remove something from something else
vocabulary.example: What he needed, in order to do it safely, was something which was not Simon himself, but something which belonged to Simon so personally that any witchcraft worked on it would work on Simon at the same time—a detachable piece of Simon, really.

vocabulary.definition: feeling afraid or anxious
vocabulary.example: He was not frightened at all now.

vocabulary.definition: a place equipped for physical exercise
vocabulary.example: Today, it was the boys’ turn in the gym.

vocabulary.definition: officials who investigate and prosecute heresy
vocabulary.example: Then, to see if this made him frightened, he thought of inquisitors.

vocabulary.definition: motivated or influenced to do something creative or positive
vocabulary.example: He was inspired.

vocabulary.definition: an innate natural impulse or tendency
vocabulary.example: But instinct told Charles that this was not the kind of magic you could work at a distance.

vocabulary.definition: the use of supernatural powers to influence events
vocabulary.example: But instinct told Charles that this was not the kind of magic you could work at a distance.

vocabulary.definition: persisting repeatedly or annoyingly
vocabulary.example: And now that he knew, it stopped nagging him.

vocabulary.definition: belonging to or affecting a particular person
vocabulary.example: It was too strong and personal.

vocabulary.definition: small amount of money given regularly to a child by their parents
vocabulary.example: What could he do to Simon that would be worth nearly a whole term’s pocket money?

vocabulary.definition: to say something loudly
vocabulary.example: He saw Charles and shouted something down.

vocabulary.definition: a form of words used as a magical charm or incantation
vocabulary.example: He was obviously not going to be able to stand anywhere near Simon to put the spell on him.

vocabulary.definition: at a loss; puzzled or confused
vocabulary.example: Charles was quite stumped at first.

vocabulary.definition: to experience something bad or unpleasant
vocabulary.example: He wanted Simon to suffer.

vocabulary.definition: to be appropriate or acceptable for
vocabulary.example: They all thought the witch was Nan Pilgrim, because of her name, which suited Charles very well.
